name: Paul Bley
description: Canadian free jazz, post-bop pianist and keyboardist (1932-2016)
abstract: Paul Bley, CM (November 10, 1932 – January 3, 2016) was a Canadian jazz pianist known for his contributions to the free jazz movement of the 1960s as well as his innovations and influence on trio playing and his early live performance on the Moog and ARP synthesizers. His music has been described by Ben Ratliff of the New York Times as "…

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: New Jazz at Carnegie

about_quotes.speaker: Howard Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: But at this jam session was Roland Kirk, Carla Bley, who started using the tuba for everything shortly after that, Paul Bley was there too.
about_quotes.doc_title: Howard Johnson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Nat Hentoff
about_quotes.evidence: But otherwise I mean Paul Bley wrote a book and he said, “I wish A&R people did what Hentoff does, he just reads the newspapers.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Nat Hentoff interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes
about_quotes.evidence: And Cecil Taylor, Bill Dixon and Carla Bley and Paul Bley, and different people like that. They started that October Revolution
about_quotes.doc_title: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 30, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: Roger Kellaway
about_quotes.evidence: The next group he had was Paul Bley and Steve Swallow. And that was, he wanted a band of composers. That happened to me twice.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roger Kellaway interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: John Abercrombie
about_quotes.evidence: The first records I think Paul Bley had a record, you know, and all of a sudden Manfred decided he’d like to record me.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Abercrombie interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 19, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: Bill Evans and Paul Bley were the two piano players, even though neither one of them had big reputations at the time.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
